text: Under ECOA and Regulation B, a creditor must notify an applicant of adverse action within 30 days of receiving a completed application. The notice must include the specific reasons for denial or a disclosure of the applicant's right to request reasons.

The Fair Credit Reporting Act requires that when adverse action is taken based on information in a consumer report, the creditor must provide the consumer with the name, address, and phone number of the consumer reporting agency that furnished the report.

During origination, the creditor must collect a completed application including the applicant's name, address, income, and the amount and purpose of the credit requested. Identity verification must be completed per CIP requirements under BSA.

Credit scoring models used in underwriting must be validated annually. The model must not use prohibited basis characteristics under ECOA including race, color, religion, national origin, sex, marital status, or age.

Under Regulation E, a financial institution must investigate a consumer's notice of error within 10 business days and resolve the error within 45 calendar days. A provisional credit must be issued within 10 business days if the investigation cannot be completed.

The Fair Credit Billing Act requires creditors to acknowledge a billing dispute within 30 days and resolve it within two billing cycles not to exceed 90 days. During the dispute period, the creditor may not report the amount as delinquent.

Zero liability for unauthorized transactions applies when the cardholder reports the loss or theft of a card promptly. Under Visa and Mastercard network rules, zero liability is extended to all consumer debit and credit transactions.

A Suspicious Activity Report must be filed within 30 calendar days of initial detection of a suspicious transaction. The SAR must not be disclosed to the subject of the report. Filing threshold is $5,000 for known subjects and $25,000 for unknown subjects.

Under the FDCPA, a debt collector must send a written validation notice within 5 days of initial contact. The notice must state the amount of the debt, the name of the creditor, and the consumer's right to dispute the debt within 30 days.

Early-stage collections policy requires that the first outbound contact attempt be made within 5 days of an account becoming 30 days past due. Payment arrangements must be documented in the servicing system within 24 hours of agreement.

An account may be charged off after 180 days of delinquency per FFIEC guidelines. Prior to charge-off, the servicer must send a final demand letter with a 30-day cure period. Charged-off accounts must be reported to credit bureaus within 30 days.

Debt sale to third-party collectors requires a purchase and sale agreement that includes representations and warranties about the accuracy of account data. The original creditor must provide a data file with payment history, last payment date, and charge-off balance.

Periodic statements must be sent at least 21 days before the payment due date under the CARD Act. The statement must include the minimum payment, the due date, and the late payment fee that will be imposed if payment is not received by the due date.

A payoff statement must be provided within a reasonable time, not to exceed 7 business days from the request. The payoff amount must include all accrued interest, fees, and any prepayment penalty disclosed in the original agreement.

Income verification during underwriting must include at minimum two recent pay stubs or the most recent W-2. Self-employed applicants must provide two years of tax returns. All income documentation must be dated within 90 days of application.

The debt-to-income ratio limit for prime credit products is 43 percent. Exceptions above 43 percent require senior credit officer approval and must be documented with compensating factors including high credit score or significant liquid assets.

Adverse action notices must be provided in writing and must not be sent electronically unless the applicant has consented to electronic delivery under E-SIGN. The notice must be clear and conspicuous and written in plain language.

When a dispute is received in writing, the servicer must acknowledge receipt within 5 business days. The investigation must be completed and a written response provided within 30 business days, extendable to 45 days with notice to the consumer.

Fraud alerts placed by consumers must be honored for a minimum of one year. An extended fraud alert lasts seven years and requires the creditor to contact the consumer directly before extending credit.
FDCPA prohibits debt collectors from calling before 8am or after 9pm local time. Collectors may not use obscene language, make false representations, or threaten actions they cannot legally take.

Credit limit increases must be based on a review of the customer's current income, payment history, and updated credit score. Unsolicited credit limit increases require an opt-out notice to be sent 45 days in advance.

The application intake process must include a disclosure of the Annual Percentage Rate, fees, and key terms before the applicant submits the application. This is required under TILA and Regulation Z for open-end credit products.

When a consumer disputes the accuracy of information reported to a credit bureau, the furnisher must conduct a reasonable investigation within 30 days and correct or delete inaccurate information. The furnisher must notify all CRAs of the correction.

Account closure initiated by the creditor requires 45 days advance notice under the CARD Act. The notice must inform the cardholder of their right to pay off the existing balance under the current terms.

Military Lending Act caps the Military Annual Percentage Rate at 36 percent for covered borrowers. Creditors must check the MLA database or use a consumer report to determine covered borrower status before consummation.
This policy has been superseded by Collections SOP v2.2. Early collections contact timing was previously 7 days after 30-day delinquency. Updated policy requires contact within 5 days.

Regulation Z requires that for credit card accounts, the minimum payment must be calculated to pay off the balance within a reasonable period. The statement must include a minimum payment warning showing the time and total cost to pay off the balance paying only minimums.

Servicemembers Civil Relief Act requires creditors to reduce the interest rate to 6 percent for obligations incurred before active duty. The servicemember must provide written notice and a copy of military orders within 180 days of release from active duty.

When underwriting a joint application, the creditworthiness of each applicant must be evaluated independently. A creditor may not require a spouse to co-sign unless the applicant does not individually qualify under the creditor's standards.

The CFPB's Unfair Deceptive or Abusive Acts or Practices standard applies to all consumer financial products. An act is unfair if it causes substantial injury to consumers that is not reasonably avoidable and not outweighed by countervailing benefits.
